sentences.text: Yes.
I-- as I said before, I was always very interested in politics.
And I was reading the papers.
And I remember, with the election in Germany, and-- when Hitler came into power and how the whole world was apprehended.
It felt very bad about and-- what's coming, especially the Jewish population.
And when the came-- time came in 1938, when the [INAUDIBLE] came in-- first, before [INAUDIBLE] was Sudetenland, which was next to-- to Germany.
And then-- then [INAUDIBLE] when Hitler and Germany invaded, actually, the-- Austria.
And then it was Czechoslovakia when they actually gave up on Czechoslovakia when [INAUDIBLE] went to-- to Munich.
And everybody-- we expected war at that time.
Yes, we had a radio.
Yes.
Actually, just before the war broke out, I was, at that time, 20 years old.
And I and my friends went for outing in the mountains, Zakopane, and to go to mountain climbing-- climbing.
And it was about two or three weeks-- two weeks, I would say, before the war.
And I remember when the-- when the army came over and-- the Polish army and sent us home.
They said, listen, you better go home, because the-- the conditions are pretty bad.
And they expected that the war would come out-- that Germany would invade pretty soon.
So we went home.
And-- and when the war started, it was, I believe, the 1st of September, 1939.
I-- we decided-- first of all, it was a call on the radio that all able-bodied people-- for young people-- should go up to the army.
And they were hoping that they are going to make a stand somewhere later on.
And of course, I was-- in Poland, one had to be 21 to go to the-- to be drafted into the army.
And I was not 21 yet.
I was 20.
So I went up to the army.
And we decided, actually, the family that it was unsafe to stay.
And so I went.
And I was walking through-- all the way to-- all the way to Dubno, which was not far from Russian border, which was quite a few hundred kilometers.
Took me about a week, I would say.
And all the time, the-- the Germans, they were invading.
And they were progressing pretty fast.
And finally, when I arrived to Dubno, that was when they-- the Germany and Russia came to an understanding.
And they decided to divide Poland and a ceasefire was announced.
And I was stuck in the Russian territory.
And I went-- after-- I remember, after, that we decided to go to a bigger city, Lvov.
And-- but they wouldn't let us in to Lvov.
It was-- fighting was going on.
And somehow, they didn't know-- there was no transportation to Lvov.
So we went around somehow.
And we got to-- to Stryi, which was south of Lvov.
I think it was south of Lvov.
And I remember with one another boy like this-- man my age, we arrived to Stryi and-- with a hook-- hook sack on our back and a few things that we came-- took with us.
And somebody came over and said, what are you doing and where are you from?
And-- and we said-- so-- I said from Tarnow.
So this man said, oh, Tarnow.
I used to work in Tarnow for many, many years.
He was a plumber.
And so I said, where-- where did you work?
So he said-- he mentioned a name of my uncle, which I never met because he died due to diphtheria epidemic in 1918.
But he-- he remembered him, and he took me to the-- to his house and-- and took me to-- actually, later, I was living with his daughter's house, married daughter's house.
And I was there till-- for about two or three weeks.
I don't know exactly when.
And there was a fellow who was coming in who was a-- a Russian army.
He was-- I don't know what rank he had.
He had a-- a few-- some-- a few things on the-- on his lapel.
I don't know what.
And he was coming.
And he was coming as a suitor to-- to his sister of-- who was living with her, too, who was the oldest sister.
And he was coming.
We got very friendly.
And naturally, I started to talk politic with him.
And I said that was no democracy there.
How is that?
Stalin this, and Stalin that.
And all of a sudden, he said you are a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
In other words, you're an enemy of the people.
So I-- I decided-- next morning, I decided to go.
And I went to Lvov.
And I was there for a few days.
My friend had a-- lived in an apartment-- rented apartment.
And I rented one-- one room for a few nights.
And-- and then I met another fellow from Tarnow who was going from-- to the border.
And-- and then we decided that-- I used to play ping pong with him before the war.
So he took me over.
And I told him I didn't have money, said my father would pay for him-- for me.
And that's what happened.
I went back to-- because the whole family-- at that time, the condition were not that bad in the beginning somehow.
When the Germans came in, I understand, because I was not there, they-- they-- they were pretty rough.
And they burned the-- the-- the synagogue.
When someone-- when I came in, they synagogue was completely destroyed.
We had a very big synagogue, one of the biggest in-- in Poland with a big steel-- steel dome.
And they destroyed it completely.
And-- so-- But they let people-- they were not organized yet.
They still-- the people had to-- they couldn't walk on the sidewalk.
They couldn't walk-- they couldn't do business-- they-- be in business.
But somehow, it was no-- they didn't-- they were shooting once in awhile, but it was-- it was not organized yet.
Anyway, I went back.
I-- I thought that they wanted to send us back to Russia in-- at that time.
And there was registration to go to Russia.
And they were sending to the-- to the-- people were saying that they are sending to the coal mines in-- in Ukraine.
And I decided to go back to-- to the family.
And I came in the beginning of January, 1940, back to Tarnow.
You know, the first-- the first impression was very scary because everybody had to wear this-- this armband to-- in order-- to identify that you are Jewish.
And you couldn't walk on the sidewalk.
You couldn't walk here.
So it was pretty scary.
But I guess I slowly acclimatized as long as you didn't-- they didn't-- at that time, they didn't kill yet, so-- I mean, it was not organized killing.
It was sporadic.
I started-- my-- my brother-in-law, my sister's husband, was a dentist.
And everybody was supposed to work.
Everybody was supposed to be occupied.
And you're getting-- when you-- and in order-- you had to get the stamp that you are working.
So I-- I was working.
I started to work and learn that little bit profession, sort of.
And that's what I was doing, actually, for-- for-- till the ghetto.
It was slowly.
It was not-- it was sort of a progressive.
They were catching people on the street they needed to work.
And you never knew if you were going on the street, if you're going to come back without sending away somewhere, some kind of camp.
They had all kinds of camps, working camps.
And you heard stories.
This one was caught.
And one was caught.
And sometimes, somebody came back, and you heard what was going on.
But it was not killing at that time, not organized killing.
But it was sort of a-- a-- whenever somebody wanted to do, they just took away from the street, and that's it.
And once in a while, you-- you heard people-- somebody-- you know, the-- the soldiers or the SS people were going on the-- on the-- street.
And day he didn't like somebody, just took out the gun, and he shot them.
But that was not organized.
It was sporadic.
Depends on the person, who he was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gestapo was always looking for somebody.

And for instance, that they knew that I came in from-- from other side, from-- from-- from Russian side, I certainly would be picked up and probably killed because I know a lot of friends of mine who came from the other side.
And they were picked up, and they never heard from them.
The ghetto was formed 1942.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] actually, it was-- they were talking about it, that it's going to be formed before.
And everybody prepared themselves.
But they didn't have enough-- enough space to put all of those people because they wanted to put in a small part of Tarnow.
And they took the-- the worst-- where the poor people were living mostly.
And before they formed the ghetto, they made the first action.

And they-- they announced that able-bodied man had to come and register to come in the morning and be-- whoever didn't work, who didn't-- whoever didn't-- because I was working, for instance, for the-- for my brother-in-law.
But that was not necessary.
That was not something that I could show that I'm working because it was not considered worthwhile work.
And so they were talking that there are going to be ghettos.
Somehow, people had some kind of a connection.
And one found out.
And everybody-- a rumor started.
There's going to be an action.
A ghetto in Krakow was already in place.
So and-- so-- I remember I didn't have a-- a working place-- a working stamp, a real good working stamp.
So they-- and my-- and my-- everybody was trying to find out because they were getting [INAUDIBLE] cards at that time, an official thing that you are really working.
So my sister started-- we had a cousin who was working as-- in the-- in the-- it was established, a police, sort of a Jewish police.
And they were watching the-- the people, how-- like they have to tell people not to walk on the sidewalk or not to walk here, not to walk there, and-- small things.
And so they established like an auxiliary that-- so my sister got me, somehow, a stamp that I could work like this.
And-- and that was the-- right after that was-- they started to get the-- the whole town-- they-- they came out with the announcement that everybody-- everybody had to go.
I don't know.
They had a list who had to come back, who come, who had to go and-- downstairs at certain time and certain day and to be in front of the house.
And-- and they are going to be sent out to a work camp somewhere.
And everybody thought that that is what it's, of course, going to be.
Everybody had to-- to take-- they told you what you-- what you had to take with you, you know, the blanket, and this and that, like to go to work.
To-- and that was in-- and they took those people.
And they-- most of them shot quite a few thousand outside of town-- outside of town.
And the rest they send out somewhere.
Nobody heard of them anymore.
Quite a few thousand were shot because it was-- and Tarnow was the place, I think, that-- I-- I don't-- they said it was [INAUDIBLE].
That's what it was.
Some [INAUDIBLE] somewhere, that-- that's where people were shot.
I didn't see it.
But that's where it was.
But the-- the main thing is most people who were-- who were not shot, they were shipped out.
And nobody heard about anybody.
No, nobody knew.
If somebody would know, you wouldn't go down with blankets and all kinds of things.
I mean, it was the first-- the first time that we heard about that somebody-- that it was organized killing.
Now, after that, after their action, there was lot of-- lot of new space for people.
And then they established ghetto.
They-- whoever left-- leftover had to move to a new-- to a new place.
And--
Yes, we moved.
We moved to [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE].
We had a room there.
Well, whatever you could.
You couldn't take too much because we didn't have enough space.
We had only one room for the whole family.
And at that time, we had living-- my uncle and aunt was living with us.
They came from Krakow.
Because we're always live-- it was so close.
And being that they established ghetto-- they were talking about before that they would establish ghetto in-- in Krakow.
So they came over because the ghetto was not in Tarnow yet.
So they lived with us.
And that's what it was.
We lived in a small-- in a big room, one family.
Yeah.
Did you work in the ghetto?
In the ghetto, I was in this-- in this auxiliary police.
And I remember we-- they were cleaning up from the people who were-- who were sent out.
And there was furniture.
And so they had a warehouse, furniture in a warehouse.
And-- and I was always watching at night.
Everybody-- it was-- Germans had to have always people to watch these things, 24 hours a day.
So I was watching the warehouse most of the time, where the furniture was.
No.
Unless you had-- you had official duty somewhere.
I mean, it was-- you had to justify your leaving the ghetto.
You-- there were working places outside of ghetto, like [INAUDIBLE], all kind of-- all kind of factories.
And people were going.
And there went in the morning, and then they came back-- back in the evening after work.
And they had to be escorted.
No.
My father's flour mill, he gave up in-- during the Depression.
After the-- Already, when I remember, we had only the-- the warehouse when we sell-- sold the merchandise.
And when the war started, actually, we sold out everything because we're not allowed to have a-- a business, especially this kind of business.
It was-- and that was easy to-- to sell.
And well, before the ghetto, we used to do some business.
We used to have connection where with to get some kind of merchandise.
We sold to the bakers at very, very little value.
People were working outside.
And they were always bringing food.
They took out things, and they exchanged.
And they sold, and they bought food.
It was not legal, but everybody knew.
Once in awhile, they made some kind of an inspection.
And they would hit people or they killed people when they found it.
But that's how everybody was living, because you couldn't live on what you got from-- from legally.
So as long as you had what to sell or to exchange, you had food.

As long as you were in-- in-- a town of-- I mean people in-- town that you live, and you knew people from the Polish-- Polish-- people outside, you can always-- they used to come to the place where you work, and you used to take-- barter or-- or sell and buy things.
And you-- What did you use for money?
Did you have money?
Mostly, mostly, it was barter.
You sell-- you sold what you have, things.
Some people had money.
I guess you had to have money.
Even-- even-- even when you close up-- it was closing up the ghettos, and you're dealing with things from people or your own things.
And you sold, and you exchange.
And whoever didn't have it, he was like an everyday poor.
In place and-- and time like this, not to be poor-- it was bad to be poor, too, because if you don't-- didn't have what to exchange, you didn't have what to eat.
Sporadic action when they needed some-- somewhere and the people to send out to-- to some kind of camps.
You know, they came in, and they-- mostly, they were sent out the camps, working camps.
People-- young people were sent out officially.
I don't know if they were-- you can call an action.
[INAUDIBLE] was second action.
It was three months after the first one.
That was a big action, yes.
That-- that was the time that I lost my whole family.
Yes.
Now, they surrounded the ghetto overnight.
And they brought in all kinds of people with-- SS people.
Figured they were drunk.
Everybody thought they were.
They came in to-- to people-- to-- you know, we have the-- the Jewish [? mind, ?] as you say, Jewish people who were supposed to be like a government for Jews, established by-- by the Germans.
And they didn't want to bother to do all the dirty work.
So they told them, we want this.
They always-- first of all, they always coming with money.
They needed money.
They needed gold.
They needed-- we had to give gold, whatever you had, all kind of things that was-- has any worth.
And-- but-- and they had to provide person.
They went to Jewish people who run the ghetto.
Run?
Actually, they didn't run.
They were-- they were told what to do.
And they told they had to prepare the whiskey, all kind of things.
And we all knew that something going on.
It's going to be on.
And then they got all drunk.
And-- and then we knew that-- that the action is going to be.
And after they surrounded the ghetto, they told people to come out.
And whoever-- whoever didn't come out, and they put everybody on the-- on the-- it was a big square.
And everybody had to kneel down and-- and wait there.
And there, whoever-- they-- they decided who is working where and which one is-- is necessary for the Germans to keep them because they were working in factories where they needed.
And those people were left over.
And they had to have the-- the stamp.
That's how they decided who is-- who is staying, mostly-- mostly young people.
And I remember when my-- I had a very good friend.
And he was-- they told him to kneel down on the-- on the square.
And he didn't want to kneel down.
And he hit the one SS man.
The other one shot him on the-- right there on the spot.
[INAUDIBLE].
And-- and the rest was sent out.
And my father, who was old-- at that time, old.
That time, he was 60.
And he was-- he was-- went through with one transport.
And [INAUDIBLE].
At the time, I-- it was-- they were taking the young kids, too.
And my sister had two kids.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And they-- they ran through them.
My brother, now, took one-- one girl.
My sister [? went ?] the other one.
They went.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was my worst day.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then, after that, they started to bring more people from outside.
Because the-- the small towns, they did the same thing everywhere else.
And they were liquidating several towns.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [CRYING] Anyway-- [SIGHS]
